author: @jameshoneyc
text: Hey my friend. I've welded for years. Once you've welded you never go without it again.  My tips would be. Clean the surfaces good ....rust makes things hard to weld. Hold your rod about a 45 deg angle leading to the direction your headed with the weld. Play with your heat settings. You want it hot enough to " carry " the arc. .  When you start the arc,,it should continue faily easy. Not to hot....you don't want it splattering or your rods glowing red.  I always work the rod left to right.. right to left in a saw tooth or shark tooth pattern. Weld a little further on the sides of the seam to the left of the seam then to the right. Do this tooth motion and maintain the rods tip about 1/8 inch above the surface to maintain the arc. Also at the same time you must travel along the seam in the direction your rod is leaning.  You'll get used to it.  Use your grinder to.  V. Groove the seams.  If you butt 2 pieces of metal together  V groove the seam this way the weld penetrates deep into the seam making it stronger.  Its not nearly as strong if you just weld over the top of the seam ... joint..or crack. I suppose it's a stardard????? But I use a 7018..rod it's fairly easy to weld with and has lots of strength..  watch your eyes. If you arc the rod with your helmet up and it flashes your eyes...it will wake you up with seemingly glass in your eyes... don't recommend it.  I'm sure there's other stuff but.. just ask.

author: @PJ6300gaming
text:
For a beginner hobbyist welder working on simple, non-hazardous applications, these methods are perfectly fine and I agree that there are often times where a grinder can really help when you accidentally put a little too much metal on the weld bead or have a little bit of undercut that can be ground out and fixed with an extra pass or two. HOWEVER THAT SAID, if someone is, say, planning on getting more into the field, building high stress structures, sealing high pressure containers (even 100 psi is very dangerous), repairing vehicular parts, etc, these methods can indeed lead to DIRE consequences. The reason for this is because there are many variables that have to be considered when welding that can have a detrimental affect on the strength of the joint. In the case of these methods, hardening of the base plates due to excessive heating and cooling will have the greatest effect. This is because you generate a lot of heat when welding, and the more you repetitively heat and cool a metal, the more stress you put on the micro structure and the more brittle the metal becomes. This is called "thermal shock". Now a piece of metal CAN have its micro structure "reset" if it is melted, but obviously that can't be done unless you plan to start from scratch. 

so tl;dr, use these methods with caution, but only in situations where structural stress isn't an issue.

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: I MAG welded a prototype windturbine in 1996, took me a day or two to have the whole system work properly, then instructed a Bulgarian guy to weld his biogas digester. Both worked out fine. Nicer welds with argon, protecting your weld, but you need time to set it all up. Never gone further, but respect to the real professionals, it’s not a piece of cake!

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: You really made me laugh again, you’re genius! One small remark about those grinders, they can be dangerous, turning at 10-20000 rotations per minute. There is a difference in discs to use . NEVER use a cutting disk ( very small thickness of 1-2 mm) to grind. Real grinding disks typically have a thickness 5-8mm. That’s ok to smoothe your weld. There are also the disks with lamella of grinding paper. Just thought it would be mentioned because those very fine discs can explode if you use them for grinding. I enjoy your pleasure in learning to weld though!

author: @kennedy67951
text: Robert for that kind of work your doing it really would be best to get yourself a (MEG WELDING MACHINE). You can use Flux Core or Gas Shielding for the Welding Wire. It's far easier to Weld with the Wire Welding process or (MEG) than Stick Welding. If you really want a good Welding Process then go with (TIG WELDING MACHINE). With this type of Welding you can Weld any Metals you want to. It's just my opinion Robert but maybe you should cut a slot across the bottom of your Can and add a Flat Plate so all you have to do is pull the Plate towards yourself and all the Burned contents would fall out the bottom into a catch Basin or Burn Box. Just my opinion. Thanks for sharing your knowledge with me and all willing to learn something new. P.S Robert. If you'll look at it in the way High End Grills are made, it might help you a lot. After all your not far off the Grill making.

author: @jamescunliffe9872
text: A round cast iron drain cover will survive the heat to let the ash fall to the bottom,soft pad grinding disc removes metal fast the type with flaps of abrasive rather then the type that resembles compressed carbide noodles! You could spend ages fabbing an ash removal pulling out pan but maybe it would be easier just to have a port and vacuum out but risk a meltdown if embers haven't extinguished! I've made stoves out of scrap and combined Bolting together with welding, I've seen gasification feedpipes slanting down with take off tubes that collect fractions,wet rag cooling also

author: @wethepeople1776x
text:
Hello good Sir from the Corn lands of the Midwest in America 🇺🇸 

LoL, I’ve been welding for some 30yrs now and the angle grinder is still one of my best friends. Whether it’s used with a wire wheel to prep areas before welding, or a grinding wheel to make joints fit better, or cleaning up welds after the welding. But yes, the angle grinder is still one of my best friends; along with sets of different vise grips to hold work pieces, magnetic angle blocks to help hold pieces 45-90* while tacking them together, also even using a speaker magnet on your grounding end (because some work one can’t find a good place to clamp it on). 
One thing I did want to ask (if I may) is: Do you have a dry storage box for your welding rods? Even having an air tight container with some silica packets will work. Humidity and temperature fluctuations can and will degrade the flux on you rods and in time they become junk and impossible to weld with (money out the window).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I must get some clamps that's for sure and yes I keep the weld rods in a cabinet - but you have to remember I ma really a chemist so most of the stuff I keep is reach and cossh compliant just by habit

author: @uncledodge9396
text: I've been welding for over 30 years, I make all manor of items and MY best friends are my selection of angle grinders! I have three set up ready for use, one with a 1mm thick cutting disk, one has a 40 grit flap disc and the final one will either sport a 80 grit flap disc or a wire brush.  Keep going with the welding and enjoy it! I started on stick welding but truth is I mostly use MIG now, and this is what I'd recommend to you.

replies.author: @uncledodge9396
replies.text: @benthere8051 BTW, your question about 'filler rods for gaps', Sometimes when I have a gap that needs fixing I just fill it with weld. Using mig just makes it so easy!

author: @michaelpearson5465
text:
Looks a damn sight better than the monstrosities I first made when I was a beginner. Using another electrode as extra filler is nicknamed "Texas TIG" and is sometimes used for exactly that purpose to fill a big gap.

On using a grinder, thats the tool I use most other than the welder, yea some welds are left depending on the application, but when something is being used, as a piece of furniture for example, or a piece of medical equipment the weld is often asked to be finished flush and smooth, or sometimes with a grained finish. Bottom line is, whether your a pro of a beginner your going to use a grinder at some point for some reason or another.
